1:
wĕheḥĕzîqû šeḇaʿ nāšîm bĕʾîš ʾeḥāḏ = "And seven women will seize (ḥāzaq, to grasp firmly) one man" — the sequel to 3:25-26: so many men have died in battle that seven women compete for one surviving man. Šeḇaʿ (seven) is a round number signifying completion/totality — all the women; all the remaining men are overwhelmed by demand.

leḥmēnû nōʾkal ûśimlātēnû nilbaš = "we will provide our own food (leḥem) and clothing (simlâ)" — in ANE marriage the husband provided food and clothing as fundamental obligations (cf. Exod 21:10). The women offer to waive these obligations — a sign of social desperation so extreme that the normal order of economic provision within marriage is abandoned. raq yiqqārēʾ šimmĕkā ʿālênû = "just let your name be called upon us" — to bear a man's name was to be under his protection and identity. The women seek identity and honor (kherpa = disgrace/reproach removed) even without the economic basis of marriage.
2:
ṣemaḥ YHWH = "the Branch of the LORD" — a title of the utmost Messianic importance. Ṣemaḥ (H6780) = a sprout, growth, shoot from a root. In this context it is theophoric: "the Shoot of YHWH" — not merely a branch of the Davidic line but one whose origin is attributed directly to YHWH himself. The parallel texts that deploy ṣemaḥ as a title are: Jer 23:5 ("I will raise up for David a righteous ṣemaḥ"); Jer 33:15 (same); Zech 3:8 ("my servant, the Branch"); Zech 6:12 ("Here is the man whose name is the Branch, and he will branch out from his place and build the temple"). In all these texts ṣemaḥ is clearly Messianic.

liṣbî ûlĕkāḇôd wĕpĕrî hāʾāreṣ lĕgāʾôn ûlĕtipʾāret = "beautiful (ṣĕḇî) and glorious (kāḇôd), and the fruit of the earth will be the pride (gāʾôn) and splendor (tipʾāret) of those who survive Israel." The four positive words in this verse (beauty, glory, pride, splendor) are the exact reversal of the four-fold "instead of" in 3:24 (stench, rope, baldness, sackcloth). The Branch brings the reversal of judgment and the restoration of beauty.
3:
wĕhāyâ hannôṭār bĕṣiyyôn wĕhannôṭār bîrûšālāim qādôš yēʾāmer lô = "And whoever remains (nôṭār, niphal participle of yātar = to be left over, to remain) in Zion and whoever is left in Jerusalem will be called holy (qādôš) — everyone enrolled (kātûḇ = written/registered) among the living (ḥayyîm) in Jerusalem."

Two key terms: śārîd (remnant by survival) in 1:9 is now supplemented by nôṭār (remainder, survivor). More importantly: kātûḇ laḥayyîm = "written for/among the living" — this is the first clear reference in Isaiah to a heavenly register of the living (the "book of life" motif found in Exod 32:32-33; Ps 69:28; Dan 12:1; Mal 3:16-17; Rev 3:5; 13:8; 20:12). Enrollment in this register, not ethnic survival, defines the remnant as holy.
4:
ʾim-rāḥaṣ ʾăḏōnāy ʾet ṣōʾat bĕnôt ṣiyyôn = "when the Lord has washed away (rāḥaṣ) the filth (ṣōʾâ = excrement/filth) of the daughters of Zion" — the vocabulary of vv. 18-24 (adornment of the daughters) is reversed: the judgment that stripped the women (3:16-24) becomes the cleansing of filth. Ṣōʾâ is a deliberately coarse word for moral impurity — the filth is the moral equivalent of the physical waste that defiles.

bĕrûaḥ mišpāṭ ûbĕrûaḥ bāʿēr = "by a spirit (rûaḥ) of judgment (mišpāṭ) and a spirit of burning/fire (bāʿar)." Two modes of divine cleansing: judicial (mišpāṭ — separating what belongs from what does not) and fiery refining (bāʿar — consuming the dross). The pairing anticipates the dual work of the Servant (purifying through justice) and the Spirit (baptism of fire — cf. Matt 3:11; Isa 44:3).
5:
wĕbārāʾ YHWH ʿal kol-mĕkôn har-ṣiyyôn = "Then the LORD will create (bārāʾ) over every dwelling-place on Mount Zion" — bārāʾ is the verb of divine creation from Genesis 1:1, reserved exclusively for divine creative acts with no human parallel. The new protective cloud over Zion is an act of creation in the same category as the original creation.

ʿānān wĕʿāšān yômām ûnōgah ʾēš lĕhābâ lāylâ = "a cloud by day and smoke with the glow of flaming fire at night" — the Exodus theophany re-enacted. Cloud by day / fire by night is the specific mode of divine accompaniment in the wilderness (Exod 13:21-22; 40:38; Num 9:15-23). The eschatological Zion is not a return to pre-Exodus conditions but a new Exodus: YHWH re-inhabits his people with the visible presence that accompanied them from Egypt. Kî ʿal-kol-kāḇôd ḥuppâ = "for over all the glory there is a canopy (ḥuppâ)" — ḥuppâ = bridal canopy/wedding chamber; the image of YHWH's presence as a wedding canopy over his people is an extraordinary metaphor for covenant intimacy under divine protection.
6:
wĕsukkâ tihyeh lĕṣēl-yômām mēḥōreḇ = "There will be a shelter (sukkâ) for shade (ṣēl) from the heat (ḥōreḇ) of the day" — sukkâ here closes the frame opened in 1:8 (Jerusalem was "like a sukkah in a vineyard" — exposed and temporary). There the sukkâ was the image of vulnerability; here it is the image of divine protection. The same word, reversed in meaning: from the city exposed like a flimsy harvest booth to the eschatological community sheltered under a divine sukkah.

ûlĕmisteir wĕlĕmaḥseh mizzærem ûmimmāṭār = "and a refuge (mistār) and hiding place (māḥāseh) from storm (zérem) and rain (māṭār)." The shelter from storm is both physical and eschatological. Three nouns for shelter in this verse (sukkâ, mistār, māḥāseh) answer the three images of Jerusalem's exposure in 1:8 (sukkâ, mĕlûnâ, nĕṣûrâ). The precision of the reversal is Isaiah's hallmark: the exact vocabulary of the indictment becomes the vocabulary of the promise.
